task:
Implement the CodeRabbit Coding Plan for issue #501: [Cockpit] Add Repo Status Dashboard Cards ## Instructions 1. `cd ~/clawd/slideheroes-internal-tools` 2. `git fetch origin && git checkout origin/main && git checkout -b sophie/issue-501` 3. Read the full issue: `gh issue view 501 --repo slideheroes/slideheroes-internal-tools` 4. Implement according to the Coding Plan below 5. Run the project's lint/typecheck commands before committing 6. Commit with: `git commit -m "feat: [Cockpit] Add Repo Status Dashboard Cards (#501)"` 7. Push: `git push origin sophie/issue-501` 8. Open PR: ```bash gh pr create --repo slideheroes/slideheroes-internal-tools \ --base main \ --head sophie/issue-501 \ --title "feat: [Cockpit] Add Repo Status Dashboard Cards" \ --body "Closes #501 Implemented per CodeRabbit Coding Plan." ``` **IMPORTANT:** - Do NOT push directly to main. Always work on the feature branch. - Do NOT merge the PR yourself. Just open it and push to the feature branch. - The repo auto-deploys when PRs are merged — you don't need to do anything after opening the PR. ## CodeRabbit Coding Plan ## Coding Plan ### Summary - Extract duplicated GitHub API helpers (`ghFetch`, `calculateAge`) into a shared `lib/github.ts` module for reuse by the new endpoint - Create a new `/api/github/repo-status` endpoint that aggregates issue data, linked PRs (via timeline events API), CodeRabbit comments, CI status, and merge status for three tracked repositories - Build a `RepoStatusCard` component using native `
`/`` for collapse and native `` for the issue grid, with traffic-light colored badges following established patterns - Wire the new endpoint into the Cockpit page's parallel fetch pattern and render the cards in place of the removed navigation tiles
Design Choices
Design Choice 1: PR-to-Issue Linking Strategy **Options Considered:** 1. Use the Issue Timeline API (`/issues/{number}/timeline`) and filter for `cross-referenced` events where `source.issue.pull_request` exists 2. Search PRs via the Search API with query `repo:X is:pr #{issue_number}` then verify body/title contains the reference 3. Use GitHub GraphQL API with `closingIssuesReferences` connection **Chosen Option:** 1 **Rationale:** The Timeline API is the most reliable REST approach—it captures both explicit "Development" links and cross-references from PR descriptions, and is already documented as the recommended approach for this use case.
Design Choice 2: CodeRabbit Bot Username **Options Considered:** 1. Match comments where `user.login` equals `coderabbitai[bot]` exactly 2. Match comments where `user.login` contains `coderabbit` as a substring **Chosen Option:** 2 **Rationale:** Use substring matching (`includes('coderabbit')`) to handle potential variations in bot naming while being specific enough to avoid false positives.
Design Choice 3: CodeRabbit Status Derivation **Options Considered:** 1. Parse comment body for specific keywords/phrases that indicate status 2. Use comment presence/recency as a simpler heuristic (no comments = Waiting, has comment = Feedback provided) **Chosen Option:** 2 **Rationale:** Start with the simpler heuristic—presence of any CodeRabbit comment means feedback was provided. More sophisticated parsing can be added later if needed for distinguishing "Plan created" vs "Passed" states.
💡 User Tips Regenerate the plan with different choices with `@coderabbitai `. ## Implementation Steps ### Phase 1: Backend Foundation This phase creates the shared GitHub utility module and the new API endpoint that aggregates all required data for the repo status cards.
Task 1: Extract Shared GitHub Utilities Create a centralized GitHub helper module to eliminate code duplication and provide reusable functions for the new endpoint. - Create `app/src/lib/github.ts` with the `ghFetch` function extracted verbatim from `app/src/app/api/github/activity/route.ts` (lines 37–61) - Add `calculateAge` function from the same source file (lines 63–76) returning `{ days, hours, label }` with the >7 day stale threshold - Export TypeScript interfaces for `GitHubIssue`, `GitHubPR`, and `GitHubComment` with fields needed by the repo-status endpoint - The `GitHubIssue` interface must include `assignee: { login: string } | null` which is returned by the API but not currently typed
Task 2: Create Repo Status API Endpoint Build the new API route that fetches and aggregates issue data, linked PRs, CodeRabbit comments, CI status, and merge status for each tracked repository. - Create `app/src/app/api/github/repo-status/route.ts` as a GET handler - Define the three target repositories: `slideheroes/2025slideheroes-sophie`, `slideheroes/slideheroes-internal-tools`, `slideheroes/roadmap` - Fetch open issues with labels `plan-me`, `status:in-progress`, or `status:blocked` using the GitHub Issues API - For each issue, fetch timeline events to find linked PRs (filter for `cross-referenced` events where `source.issue.pull_request` exists) - For each issue, fetch comments and filter for CodeRabbit bot comments (user.login contains 'coderabbit') - For linked PRs, extract `mergeable` status from the PR object and fetch commit status for CI via `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits/{sha}/status` - Map issue labels to Neo status: `status:in-progress` → "coding", `status:blocked` → "blocked", PR exists → "pr-open", default → "waiting" - Derive CodeRabbit status: has CodeRabbit comment → "feedback", no comment → "waiting" - Structure response as `{ repos: [{ name, owner, issues: [...] }], generatedAt }` with each issue containing all computed statuses
Task 3: Define Response Types Establish clear TypeScript types for the API response to ensure type safety between backend and frontend. - Define `RepoStatusIssue` type with fields: `number`, `title`, `url`, `codeRabbitStatus`, `neoStatus`, `pr` (nullable with `number`, `url`, `ciStatus`, `mergeable`), `age` (from `calculateAge`), `assignee` - Define `RepoStatus` type with `name`, `owner`, `fullName`, `issues` array - Define `RepoStatusResponse` type with `repos` array and `generatedAt` timestamp - Export types from the route file or a shared types location for import by the Cockpit page
🤖 Prompt for AI agents ``` This phase establishes the backend foundation by creating a shared GitHub utility module and a new aggregating API endpoint for repo status cards. **Task 1: Create `app/src/lib/github.ts`** - Extract the `ghFetch` function verbatim from `app/src/app/api/github/activity/route.ts` (lines 37–61) - Extract the `calculateAge` function from the same file (lines 63–76); it must return `{ days ## Coding Rules # Coding Rules These rules document the actual code quality patterns used in the SlideHeroes codebase. ## TypeScript Patterns - Strict mode compliance: enable all strict TypeScript options - Use explicit local type aliases rather than inline complex types - Use `z.infer` for Zod-derived types - Use `instanceof Error` narrowing in catch blocks instead of `instanceof` checks on non-Error values - Never use `any` - use `unknown` with type guards when needed ## API Route Patterns - Use Next.js 15 style with `params` as Promise - Use named exports (GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE) for route handlers - Use `.catch(() => ({}))` for body parsing error handling - Use `NextResponse.json({ error })` response shape - Validate input at boundaries using Zod schemas ## Prisma Patterns - Import Prisma client from `@/lib/db` - Use conditional spread for PATCH updates (e.g., `...(data || {})`) - Use `.catch(() => null)` for not-found handling ## Import Conventions - Use `@/` alias for src imports (e.g., `@/components/Button`) - Import from specific files, avoid barrel exports - Separate client and server imports - never mix them from the same file ## Error Handling - Use tight try/catch around specific I/O operations only - Use `logger.error({ err }, '...')` for structured logging - Import logger from `@kit/shared/logger` - Handle errors gracefully using try/catch and appropriate error types - Use `isRedirectError` from `next/dist/client/components/redirect-error` for client-side redirect handling ## React Patterns - Encapsulate repeated blocks of code into reusable local components - Write small, composable, explicit, well-named components - Always use `react-hook-form` and `@kit/ui/form` for forms - Use client components only when needed for interactivity (use `use client` directive) - Add `data-testid` for E2E tests where appropriate - Prefer server-side data fetching using RSC - Display loading indicators where appropriate ## Next.js Patterns - Use `enhanceAction` for Server Actions from `@kit/next/actions` - Use `use server` in server action files - Export page components using the `withI18n` utility - Add well-written page metadata to pages - Redirect using `redirect` following a server action instead of using client-side router ## Data Fetching Patterns - Use async server components that call loader functions for initial data fetching - Use `enhanceAction` for create/update/delete operations with schema validation - Use `Promise.all()` for parallel data fetching in Server Components ## Linting and Formatting Before committing, always run: ```bash pnpm format:fix && pnpm lint:fix && pnpm typecheck ``` ## Component Organization - Route-specific components: `_components/` directories - Route utilities: `_lib/` for client, `_lib/server/` for server-side - Global components: Root-level directories ## Naming Conventions - Pages: `page.tsx` (Next.js convention) - Loaders: `{feature}-page.loader.ts` - Actions: `{feature}-server-actions.ts` - Schemas: `{feature}.schema.ts` - Components: `kebab-case.tsx`
